coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient removal of Pb2+ and Cd2+ on MgAl-LDH modified with carbon dots, citric acid and amino silane: Kinetic, isothermal and mechanistic studies
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Zhongying
Li, Quanwei
Wei, Xiaorong
Lu, Mengyu
Chang, Zhixian
Chong, Ruifeng
Li, Deliang
coredata.dc:description: To increase active sites for Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal, MgAl-layered double hydroxide (MgAl-LDH) was modified with carbon dots (CDs), citric acid (CA) and 3-triethoxysilylpropylamine (APTES) via a step-by-step hydrothermal method. The resultant composite (LCC-NH2) exhibited excellent removal capacity of 511.5 mg/g and 166.0 mg/g for Pb2+ and Cd2+ at 298 K, which were 6-fold and 12-fold that of MgAl-LDH. Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal fits best to the pseudo-second-order kinetic and Langmuir isotherm models. And thermodynamic studies indicated the adsorption of Pb2+ and Cd2+ on LCC-NH2 was spontaneous. In a multifaceted metal system, LCC-NH2 still demonstrated superior removal capacity for Pb2+ and Cd2+. However, it showed greater selectivity for Pb2+ than Cd2+. The mechanism studies indicated the efficient removal of Pb2+ and Cd2+ was mainly attributed to the synergistic effect encompassing isomeric substitution, chelation, and precipitation. Therefore, LCC-NH2 offers considerable potential for practical applications in environmental remediation efforts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heavy metals removal
Layered double hydroxide
Multiple modification
3-triethoxysilylpropylamine

Effect of pH on removal capacity
Effect of initial concentration and adsorption isotherm

Moreover, Special thanks go to Shufang Tian from Henan University for their significant help in this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) N2 adsorption-desorption curves and (b) pore size profiles of LDH, LDH-CDs, LDH-CA-CDs, and LCC-NH2.
TG curves of LDH, LDH-CDs, LDH-CA-CDs, and LCC-NH2.

(a) Relationship between contact time of Pb2+ and Cd2+ solution and removal capacity; Adsorption kinetics of (b) Pb2+ and (c) Cd2+; (d) Relationship between initial pH Pb2+ and Cd2+ solution and removal capacity.
Relationship between initial concentration and removal capacity of LDH-CDs, LDH-CA-CDs, and LCC-NH2 for (a-c) Pb2+ and (d-f) Cd2+.
Adsorption isotherms of LDH-CDs, LDH-CA-CDs, and LCC-NH2 for (a-c) Pb2+ and (d-f) Cd2+.
Termodynamic curve of Pb2+ and Cd2+.
Removal mechanisms of LCC-NH2 toward Pb2+ and Cd2+.
Comparison of Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal capacity in mixed solutions with different concentrations.
Thermodynamic parameters of LDH-CDs, LDH-CA-CDs, and LCC-NH2.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient removal of Pb2+ and Cd2+ on MgAl-LDH modified with carbon dots, citric acid and amino silane: Kinetic, isothermal and mechanistic studies

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Protection and Safety Energy Storage for Light Metal Materials, College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Henan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To increase active sites for Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal, MgAl-layered double hydroxide (MgAl-LDH) was modified with carbon dots (CDs), citric acid (CA) and 3-triethoxysilylpropylamine (APTES) via a step-by-step hydrothermal method. The resultant composite (LCC-NH2) exhibited excellent removal capacity of 511.5 mg/g and 166.0 mg/g for Pb2+ and Cd2+ at 298 K, which were 6-fold and 12-fold that of MgAl-LDH. Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal fits best to the pseudo-second-order kinetic and Langmuir isotherm models. And thermodynamic studies indicated the adsorption of Pb2+ and Cd2+ on LCC-NH2 was spontaneous. In a multifaceted metal system, LCC-NH2 still demonstrated superior removal capacity for Pb2+ and Cd2+. However, it showed greater selectivity for Pb2+ than Cd2+. The mechanism studies indicated the efficient removal of Pb2+ and Cd2+ was mainly attributed to the synergistic effect encompassing isomeric substitution, chelation, and precipitation. Therefore, LCC-NH2 offers considerable potential for practical applications in environmental remediation efforts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Through the industrialization process, the discharge of wastewater containing Pb2+ and Cd2+ has become a prevalent issue in surface waters. Given their non-biodegradable and bioaccumulative nature, Pb2+ and Cd2+ persist in the ecological environment over extended periods, ultimately entering the human food chain [1]. The World Health Organization has established maximum contamination thresholds for Pb2+ and Cd2+ in drinking water at 0.01 and 0.003 mg/L, respectively. Elevated levels of these metals can directly precipitate health issues in humans, affecting organs such as the skin, brain, pancreas, and liver [2,3]. In response, numerous techniques have been deployed to remove Pb2+ and Cd2+ contamination in wastewater, encompassing chemical precipitation, ion exchange, adsorption, and membrane filtration. Among these methodologies, adsorption stands out as a preferred option due to its operational simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and high removal capacity [4]. Nevertheless, the pursuit of efficient and economically viable adsorbents remains a pivotal focus of ongoing research efforts.

Layered double hydroxide (LDH) represents a category of anionic clay minerals characterized by a two-dimensional layered structure. The general formula for LDH is expressed as [M2+ 1-xM2+ x (OH)2]x+(An-)x/n·mH2O]x- [5]. Due to their intercalation capacity and facile surface functionalization, LDH have been extensively used for contaminant removal. Notwithstanding these advantageous attributes, the broader application of LDH is constrained by limited binding sites, structural vulnerability, and pronounced agglomeration tendencies. Strategies such as intercalation, grafting, and amalgamation with other functional materials have been employed to enhance the performance of LDH, bolstering their competitiveness within the realm of environmental remediation [6,7].

Incorporating diverse functional materials, such as carbon-based substances, into LDH via chemical and physical synthesis techniques proves effective in mitigating LDH particle aggregation, enhancing surface functional group density, and augmenting specific surface area, thereby introducing novel functionalities to LDH [8]. Carbon dots (CDs) represent a novel class of carbon nanomaterials characterized by particle sizes smaller than 10 nm. Notably, CDs readily bind to LDH by electrostatic self-assembly process, and this synergistic integration combines the exceptional properties of LDH and CDs, resulting in superior stability and removal performance [9,10]. Consequently, the versatile properties of CDs position them as promising precursors for the development of highly efficient adsorbents. As of 2024, approximately 40 studies have explored LDH/CDs composites, with only a handful focusing on their application as adsorbents [11–15]. Intercalation capacity on the rich interlayer regions generated by inserted anions, which furnish an abundance of active sites with enhanced removal performance. Furthermore, the creation of diverse functional groups facilitates the selective adsorption of heavy metal ions [16,17]. Citric acid (CA), recognized as a biodegradable and environmentally friendly heavy metal chelator, has found extensive application as an LDH intercalating agent [18–22]. Notably, the surface of LDH features an abundance of hydroxyl groups, albeit with a weak chelating effect on heavy metals. However, owing to their reactive nature, hydroxyl groups readily undergo dehydration and dehydrogenation reactions, facilitating the attachment of inorganic or organic groups such as epoxide and silanol groups to the LDH surface, thereby augmenting active sites for Pb2+ and Cd2+ [23]. Among these, amino groups emerge as the most commonly employed grafting entities, exhibiting superior heavy metal ion chelation capacity compared to hydroxyl groups while posing minimal environmental toxicity. Through silanization reactions, ammonia molecules are anchored, enhancing both removal performance and stability [24–26]. Composite, intercalation, or grafting techniques can enhance LDH's removal capacity to some extent but may fall short of meeting actual demands. It is anticipated that adsorbents employing composite modification methods, combining two or more modification techniques, will not only boost removal capacity by increasing surface functional groups and specific surface area but also confer significant traits to LDH. However, only a handful of studies have thoroughly explored the comprehensive advantages of composite modification methods [27–32]. Aghaziarati et al. showed that LDH adsorbents modified with CDs and L-histidine exhibited higher extraction efficiency and stability of chlorophenols [28]. Jiang et al. synthesized LDH nanocages integrated with CDs-modified ultrafine metal sulfide nanoparticles to enhance conductivity and stabilize the electrode structure [29].

Therefore, this study aims to assess the removal behavior of composite LDH with CDs (LDH-CDs), CDs combined with CA intercalated LDH (LDH-CA-CDs), and CDs integrated with CA intercalated amino-grafted LDH (LCC-NH2) concerning Pb2+ and Cd2+, utilizing LDH-CDs as a substrate. The specific objectives include (1) analyzing the effects of different modification methods on LDH structure; (2) comparing the adsorption performance of LDH-CDs, LDH-CA-CDs, and LCC-NH2 on Pb2+ and Cd2+ under both single and competitive adsorption scenarios; (3) analyzing adsorption isotherm, kinetics, thermodynamic, and mechanistic.
All chemicals and reagents used in this study were described in detail in Supplementary Material.

Synthesis of LDH-CDs: In this work, 1.1539 g of Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.5625 g of Al(NO3)3·9H2O, and 1.8000 g of urea were dissolved by using deionized water, Then, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20 mL of CDs (in the Supplementary Material) was added at the same time under ultrasonication (The total volume of the resulting suspension was adjusted to 70 mL), The above solution was then transferred to a 100 mL stainless-steel Teflon lined autoclave. Subsequently, reacted in an oven at 120 ℃ for 24 h. In the end, LDH-CDs1, LDH-CDs5, LDH-CDs10, LDH-CDs15 and LDH-CDs20 were collected by suction filtration, washed thoroughly with deionized water until neutral. After that, LDH-CDs was dried at 60 ℃ for 6 h, and then grinding with an agate mortar.

LDH-CA-CDs preparation: Similar to the synthesis method of LDH-CDs, 1.1539 g Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.5625 g Al(NO3)3·9H2O, 0.3000 g citric acid, and 1.8000 g urea were dispersed in 60 mL of deionized water via ultrasonication. Meanwhile, 10 mL of the CDs solution was added. The solution obtained was transferred into a Teflon vessel. The Teflon vessel was placed in an oven from room temperature to 120 ℃ and maintained 120 ℃ for 24 h. Subsequently, the products obtained above was isolated by suction filtration, washed extensively using deionized water. The products were again dried at 60 ℃ and ground.

Preparation of LCC-NH2: 0.2000 g LDH-CA-CDs was blended uniformly with 30 mL of ethylene glycol, Then the mixture was sonicated for 1 h, after which a certain amount of APTES was dropped under ultrasonication. After adding APTES, the reaction solution was further subjected to 75 ℃ oil bath for a certain time. Next, after cooling, the above solution was suction filtration, and was washed with deionized water until the pH neutral, which was dried at 50 ℃ for a period of 6 h and labeled as LCC-NH2.

Using 2.5 mmol of Pb(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2 and 250 mL volumetric flask, 10 mmol/L Pb2+ and Cd2+ solution were prepared. After that, 1 mmol/L of Pb2+ and Cd2+ solution (1000 mL) had been obtained by diluted. 0.012 g of adsorbent were then submerged into 20 mL of heavy metal ions solution for concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 4 mmol/L, removal process was then continuously oscillated at 220 rpm for different time under atmospheric pressure and certain temperature. After removal equilibrium, the residual concentration of Pb2+ and Cd2+ were determined by an Optima DV2100 inductively coupled plasma emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES). The Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal capacity was calculated by Eq. (1).
(1)
q
e
=
(
C
0
−
C
e
)
m
·V
where q e (mg/g) is the removal capacity of Pb2+ and Cd2+; the C 0 (mg/L) and C e (mg/L) are the initial and equilibrium concentration, respectively; the V (mL) is the solution volume; the m (mg) is the mass of sorbents. All experiments were repeated three times or more. Standard deviation (SD) were listed in all tables.

The CDs were analyzed using UV–vis absorption spectra, revealing a distinctive absorption peak at 287 nm, as illustrated in the Supplementary Material (see Fig. S1). This peak arises from the n-π transition of the CO and the π-π transition of the CC. Notably, the image of the CDs solution in the upper right corner showcases excellent water dispersion, indicative of successful synthesis. Furthermore, upon light irradiation, the solution exhibits the "Tyndall effect," confirming the successful synthesis of the nanoscale CDs solution.

The materials underwent XRD analysis to assess their purity and structure pre- and post-modification. As depicted in the Supplementary Material (Figs. S2a and 2b), LDH, LDH-CDs, LDH-CA-CDs, and LCC-NH2 exhibit characteristic peaks centered at 2θ of 11.6°, 23.4°, 34.9°, 39.5°, and 46.9°, corresponding to the (003), (006), (012), (015), and (018) planes of LDH, aligning with the standard card for MgAl-LDH (PDF#89–0460). The distinct diffraction peaks affirm the high crystallinity and purity of the prepared materials. Notably, the structure remained unchanged upon CDs addition. However, as LDH complexes with CDs during initial growth, peak intensity gradually decreases, impacting subsequent growth. Following CA intercalation, the (003) peak indicative of interlayer spacing shifts leftward, with LDH-CA-CDs (1.19 nm) exhibiting increased interlayer spacing compared to LDH-CDs (0.76 nm) as per Bragg's law. LCC-NH2 peak intensity diminishes significantly due to amino group grafting, yet the LDH structure remains intact. In the Supplementary Material (Fig. S2c), all characteristic diffraction peaks exhibit a trend of initial decrease, followed by an increase and subsequent decrease with increasing coupling agent addition. This behavior results from preferential amino group grafting on the LDH's outer surface, with some grafted onto the inner surface, leading to maximum crystallinity at a coupling agent addition of 0.6 mL. Further addition reduces crystallinity as more coupling agent interacts with LDH surface. Fig. S2d (in the Supplementary Material) illustrates that grafting time doesn't affect diffraction peak positions; however, intensities corresponding to 2θ = 7.4° increase with reaction time, favoring amino grafting on LDH's inner surface over time.

Furthermore, the morphological structure of the prepared samples was assessed through SEM analysis. As illustrated in the Supplementary Material (Fig. S3), the SEM images unveil a characteristic hexagonal sheet morphology, approximately 1200 nm in size, with LDH thickness measuring around 100 nm. The material morphologies with varying CDs amounts was comparatively studied in the Supplementary Material (Fig. S4) to enhance active site availability. At a CDs solution volume of 10 mL, LDH-CDs exhibited irregular nanosheets, dispersed particles, and minimal size, while largely retaining a layered structure. However, CDs quantities increased, LDH-CDs displayed noticeable agglomeration, with the regular hexagonal lamellar morphology diminishing, corroborating XRD findings. Fig. S5a in the Supplementary Material also provides their removal experiments. Following CA intercalation, LDH-CA-CDs displayed pronounced agglomeration compared to the precursor, with an irregular morphology yet retaining two-dimensional nanosheets shape. Post-introduction of amino groups, SEM images across varied coupling agent additions and grafting times exhibited no significant differences, yet all showcased increased agglomeration in the Supplementary Material (Fig. S6).

Hence, to maximize removal active sites, as shown in the Supplementary Material (Figs. S5b and 5c), we investigated the removal effect of various grafting conditions on Pb2+ and Cd2+. Optimal removal capacity occurred with a coupling agent addition of 0.6 mL and a coupling time of 12 h. Consequently, we adopted this condition for our subsequent experiments.

For a deeper understanding of the impact of amino introduction into LDH-CA-CDs on its constituent elements, we employed EDS to analyze the elemental composition of the material. As depicted in the Supplementary Material (Fig. S7), the EDS results exhibit a uniform distribution of the elements C, O, Mg, and Al. Following amino grafting, the emergence of the Si element signifies the condensation reaction between the silane coupling agent and LDH-CA-CDs, thereby confirming the successful grafting of amino groups onto LDH-CA-CDs.

The functional groups of the synthesized adsorbents were analyzed via FT-IR spectroscopy, as depicted in the Supplementary Material (Fig. S8). The broad absorption peak at 3439 cm−1 was attributed to the O-H stretching vibration of hydroxide layers and interlayer H2O, with the broadness indicating the presence of hydrogen bonds to varying degrees. Peaks within the 800–600 cm−1 range were associated with M–O, O–M–O, and M–O–M stretching in the MgAl-LDH lattice [33]. The absorption band at 1382 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of nitrate anion. Peaks at 1632 cm−1 and 1486 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of –CO and –CC, respectively, indicating the compounding of CDs on the LDH [14]. The –OH stretching vibration peak shifted to 3454 cm−1, suggesting stronger bonding forces between CDs and LDH laminate, such as electrostatic interaction and metallic bond formation, enhancing LDH-CDs stability. Notably, the characteristic absorption peak of NO3 - vanished, while –COO- peaks from CA appeared at 1398 cm−1 and 1632 cm−1, confirming successful CA intercalation in the LDH interlayer region. Introduction of amino groups led to peaks at 2933 cm−1 (C-H stretching vibration) and around 1581 cm−1 (NH2 bending vibration). Peaks associated with Si-O-Si absorption (1053 cm−1 and 1392 cm−1) and Si–O–M vibration (965 cm−1) indicated successful APTES grafting onto the laminate surface through a dehydration reaction between APTES hydrolyzed –OH and laminate surface –OH groups [34].

N2 physisorption measurements were performed on the as-prepared products to analyze their porous structure, surface area, and pore size distribution ( Fig. 1). Following the IUPAC classification in 2015, all products exhibited type IV isotherms with a distinct H3-type hysteresis loop at P/P0 between 0.4 and 0.99, indicating the presence of mesopores. Notably, as depicted in the Supplementary Material (Table S1), LDH-CDs and LDH-CA-CDs displayed higher specific surface areas than MgAl-LDH, which was related to the increased removal of Pb2+ and Cd2+. Furthermore, the addition of both CDs and CA altered the pore size distribution of LDH, with LDH-CA-CDs exhibiting an increased main pore size distribution from 3.8 nm to 15 nm due to CA's larger molecular structure. However, post-amino grafting, the pore size distribution above 35 nm notably increased, likely due to particle stacking, evidenced by reduced pore size distribution below 5 nm. Additionally, agglomeration induced by condensed APTEs grafting led to a significant decrease in specific surface area and pore volume of LCC-NH2, consistent with SEM observations. While reduced specific surface area may impact removal capacity for heavy metal ions, amino group grafting aids in Pb2+ and Cd2+ chelation, necessitating optimization of synthesis conditions to mitigate unfavorable factors.

Thermal stability studies of the prepared materials were conducted using TGA curves. As depicted in Fig. 2, the weight loss of the three adsorbents primarily occurs in two stages. Initially, below approximately 200 ℃, LDH, LDH-CDs, and LDH-CA-CDs exhibit a similar first step, which attributed to the removal of interlayer water and surface-adsorbed water, with materials retaining layered structure at this stage [35]. The second step, occurring between 300 and 580 ℃ for LDH, LDH-CDs, and LDH-CA-CDs, likely involves layer dehydroxylation and intercalation anion (NO3 - or CA) decomposition, leading to LDH skeleton collapse [36]. Following CDs addition, the mass loss in the first step slightly increased due to the introduction of more water molecules, yet LDH-CDs exhibited significantly lower mass loss than MgAl-LDH until 580 ℃, indicating superior thermal stability under experimental conditions. Conversely, after CA intercalation, the second step's mass loss was notably higher due to CA's poor thermal stability. Temperatures exceeding 305 ℃ likely involve layer dehydroxylation, CA decomposition, and amino silane carbon chain decomposition [24]. Noteworthy is the higher decomposition temperature of LCC-NH2's second stage compared to LDH-CA-CDs, suggesting LCC-NH2 possesses stronger thermal stability, possibly attributed to stronger surface contacts resulting from APTES bonding with LDH-CA-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of contact time and adsorption kinetics
ce:section.ce:para:
The equilibrium time for adsorbents is a critical factor influencing removal performance, and investigating adsorption kinetics provides insights into reaction pathways and rate control steps. To comprehend the removal mechanism, we examined the effect of contact time on performance and fitted experimental data using kinetic models. The equations for pseudo-first-order (PFO) and pseudo-second-order (PSO) models are represented by Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively. Here, q e and q t (mmol/g) denote the Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal performance per unit mass of adsorbent at equilibrium and time t, respectively. k 1 (min−1) and k 2 (g/mmol·min) represent the first and second order adsorption rate constants, respectively. Additionally, when the t value approaches 0, the initial sorption rate H 0 (mmol/g·min) is calculated according to Eq. (4).
(2)
q
t
=
q
e
( 1 −
e
− t
k
1
)
(3)
q
e
=
k
2
q
e
2
t
1 +
k
2
q
e
t
(4)
H
0
=
k
2
q
e
2

Fig. 3a illustrate the consistent effect of contact time of the three adsorbents on Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal. The process can be delineated into three stages: initially, within the first 60 min, the removal of Pb2+ and Cd2+ increases rapidly due to abundant active sites on the adsorbents' outer surface and a relatively large concentration gradient of metal ions between the solution and the adsorbent surface. Subsequently (60–240 min), removal process occurs at a slower rate as outer surface sites become gradually occupied, prompting diffusion of heavy metal ions to the inner surface. Additionally, due to repulsive forces between heavy metal ions on the adsorbent surface and those in the solution, remaining active sites are less easily occupied, resulting in a slower removal rate and a gradual approach to equilibrium. Finally, the removal equilibrium (240–600 min) is attained. Notably, the equilibrium removal capacity follow the order: Pb2+ > Cd2+, attributed to the inherent characteristics of metal ions; for instance, Pb2+ exhibits higher electronegativity, thus displaying greater affinity for most functional groups in the adsorbent material.

The value of relevant parameters of both kinetic models are presented in the Supplementary Material (Table S2 and S3). Notably, the PSO model demonstrates a higher correlation coefficient compared to the PFO model, indicating that adsorption primarily involves chemisorption or chemical bonding between heavy metal ions and adsorbent functional groups. Regarding H0 values for Pb2+ and Cd2+ across the three adsorbents, Pb2+ exhibits significantly higher H0 than Cd2+, implying a faster adsorption rate for Pb2+. This discrepancy may stem from Pb2+ being a hard Lewis acid, exhibiting strong affinity for functional groups like -COOH and -OH found in organic matter (hard Lewis base) [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on removal capacity
ce:section.ce:para: The pH impacts the physical and chemical properties of both adsorbent and adsorbate, along with the morphology of heavy metal cations. Considering the formation of metal ion precipitates and the instability of LDH at pH levels below 2, the pH range for Pb2+ removal was set from 2 to 6, while for Cd2+ removal, it was determined to be from 2 to 8 [8,12,38]. Fig. 3d illustrates that the removal capacity of Pb2+ and Cd2+ increases with the solution pH. Variations in surface charges of adsorbents at different pH levels may account for this trend. Several factors contribute to the lower removal capacity under acidic conditions: (i) competitive adsorption between H+ and heavy metal cations occurs at lower pH levels; (ii) the layered structure may undergo slight dissolution; (iii) the high concentration of H+ ions in acidic pH leads to increased protonation of abundant functional groups such as -COO-, -NH2, and -OH on the adsorbent surface. Compared with the -COO- functional group, -COOH exhibits a weaker adsorption effect on Pb2+ and Cd2+, while NH3 + causes electrostatic repulsion with Pb2+ and Cd2+, resulting in lower removal capacity [39]. The sudden increase in Cd2+ removal capacity at pH 8 could be attributed to hydroxide precipitation formation. Moreover, the minimal variation in LCC-NH2 removal capacity between pH 3 and 6 suggests its practical efficacy in treating heavy metal-contaminated wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial concentration and adsorption isotherm
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4 depicts the impact of various initial concentrations on the removal capacity of Pb2+ and Cd2+ at each temperature. The adsorption isotherm curves offer insights into the equilibrium state of the adsorbate and the adsorbent in solution, elucidating potential adsorption mechanisms [40]. Experimental data were fitted and analyzed using Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models (Eqs. (5) and (6)). Here, q e (mmol/g) denotes the equilibrium removal capacity; C e (mmol/L) stands for the equilibrium concentration; q m (mmol/g) represents the maximum removal capacity; K L (L/mmol) and K F [(mmol/g)/(mmol/L)1/n], 1/n represent the Langmuir adsorption constant and Freundlich constant, respectively; while 1/n is a constant associated with the Freundlich equation ( Fig. 5).
(5)
q
e
=
K
L
q
m
C
e
1 +
K
L
C
e
(6)
q
e
=
K
F
C
e
1 / n

The removal capacity of Pb2+ and Cd2+ on three adsorbents sharply increases with rising initial concentration within the range of 0.1–4 mmol/L, driven by the strong concentration gradient between the adsorbent and adsorbate surfaces. Subsequently, equilibrium is reached as available active sites gradually saturate. Interaction with CA resulted in an increase in LDH group spacing from 0.76 nm to 1.19 nm and a rise in specific surface area from 33.20 m2/g to 44.13 m2/g. Consequently, the saturated removal capacity of Pb2+ and Cd2+ on LDH-CA-CDs surpassed that of LDH-CDs. Moreover, post-grafting with APTES, the LDH layer became adorned with numerous -NH2 groups. The acidic solution's pH favored -NH2 protonation, further enhancing the adsorbent's performance. The saturated removal capacity of LCC-NH2 for Pb2+ and Cd2+ were 2.469 mmol/g (511.5 mg/g) and 1.4769 mmol/g (166.0 mg/g), respectively (temperature 298 K). Remarkably, LCC-NH2 exhibited significantly higher removal capacity for Pb2+ and Cd2+ compared to the other two adsorbents. Meanwhile, the removal capacity increased with rising solution temperature, indicating an endothermic process. Elevated temperatures augment the kinetic energy of adsorbent particles and the mobility of heavy metal ions, increasing collision frequency between adsorbent and heavy metals. Moreover, higher temperatures may expedite slow adsorption steps or generate new active sites on the adsorbent surface, thus enhancing removal [46].

The Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherm models were employed to fit the data, with results presented in the Supplementary Material (Table S4 and S5). Remarkably, the adsorption curves of Pb2+ and Cd2+ on LDH-CDs, LDH-CA-CDs, and LCC-NH2 are better simulated with the Langmuir model, indicating that Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal as mono-molecular layers on adsorbent surfaces. Furthermore, chemical adsorption is usually consistent with the Langmuir isotherm model, which is consistent with the conclusion of kinetic experiment. Owing to the synergistic effect of various modification methods, the maximum removal capacity of Pb2+ and Cd2+ follow the order: LCC-NH2 > LDH-CA-CDs > LDH-CDs.

Thermodynamics assess the temperature-dependent nature of adsorption, its spontaneity, energy, and driving force. The enthalpy change (ΔH 0), Gibbs free energy change (ΔG 0), and entropy (ΔS 0) derived from Eqs. (7–9) are summarized in Table 1 [41]. Positive ΔH 0 values affirm that the adsorption process is endothermic, consistent with earlier experimental findings. In addition, the absolute values of ΔH 0 ranged between 20 KJ/mol and 80 KJ/mol, (experimental results ranged from 50 KJ/mol - 65 KJ/mol), suggesting that the process involves both physisorption and chemisorption, and that chemisorption is dominant [42]. Positive ΔS 0 values across all removal processes indicate increased disorder at the adsorbent-liquid interface during adsorption migration of Pb2+ and Cd2+ from solution to the material surface elevates system entropy [43]. Additionally, negative ΔG 0 values suggest that Pb2+ and Cd2+ adsorption onto the absorbent is spontaneous within the temperature range of 15–35°C. The increasingly negative ΔG 0 with rising temperature further underscores the endothermic nature of adsorption. Notably, the more negativeΔG 0 values for heavy metals adsorbed by LCC-NH2 suggest that removing heavy metals with LCC-NH2 is comparatively easier, followed by LDH-CA-CDs and LDH-CDs. Moreover, the ΔG 0 values across the three adsorbents generally show Pb2+ < Cd2+, indicating that Pb2+ is more readily adsorbed than Cd2+. This discrepancy is attributed to their properties, such as the smaller hydration radius of Pb2+, resulting in less resistance to movement ( Fig. 6).
(7)
K
e
0
= 55.5 * 1000 *
K
L
(8)
∆ G
0
= − RT ln
K
e
0
(9)
K
e
0
= exp [
∆ S
0
R
−
∆ H
0
R T
]
Where, K e 0 is the thermodynamic constant (dimensionless), R is the gas constant 8.314 J / (mol·k), T is the absolute temperature Kelvin.

Multi-mechanisms are often concurrently at play in the removal process on LDH composites, encompassing isomorphic substitution, surface adsorption, anion exchange, chemical precipitation, and coordination [5,16]. The kinetics and isotherms of Pb2+ and Cd2+ adsorption by LCC-NH2 suggest that the predominant removal mechanism involves chemisorption of a monolayer. Further analysis using XRD, SEM, and FT-IR sheds light on the removal mechanism. As depicted in the Supplementary Material (Figs. S9 and S10), Despite the retention of characteristic diffraction peaks of LDH at (003), (006), (012), (015), and (018) following Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal by LCC-NH2, the peak intensities diminish. Isomorphic substitution, a hallmark of LDH, is more likely to occur when the radius of the substituting ion approaches that of Mg2+. Cd2+ closely aligns with Mg2+, better preserving the LDH structure post-Cd2+ adsorption [16]. Conversely, the appearance of new peaks after Pb2+ removal could be attributed to Pb3(CO3)2(OH)2 formation, arising from OH- from MgAl-LDH dissolution and CO3 2- from the removal process [44]. A slight leftward shift in the (003) peak indicates the formation of larger interlayer anions (M(C6H5O7)-, where M represents Pb2+ or Cd2+), attributed to the smaller diameters of Pb2+ and Cd2+ compared to the LDH pore size. FT-IR analysis reveals a minor shift in peaks associated with -OH and -NH2 groups, implicating their involvement in heavy metal removal. Pb2+ and Cd2+ coordinate with atoms in functional groups (e.g., O and N) via lone pair electrons, as indicated by characteristic peak shifts [38,45]. Morphological analysis and EDS mapping of LCC-NH2 post-Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal confirm successful bonding, with blocky structures attributed to Pb3(CO3)2(OH)2 formation after Pb2+ removal, while Cd2+ removal induces minimal morphological change (Fig. S8). In addition, physical adsorption is one of the common mechanisms in porous materials [46]. The main schematic representation of removal mechanism are shown in Fig. 7.

To explore the impact of natural and industrial wastewater heavy metal variations, we investigated the competitive effect of Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+ on Pb2+ and Cd2+. As shown in Fig. 8, experimental conditions involved Pb2+ or Cd2+ concentrations at 1 mmol/L and interfering ion concentrations at 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, and 1 mmol/L. Due to the similar electron distribution and charge overlap among Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+, the removal capacity of Pb2+ and Cd2+ in multivariate solutions decreased compared to single metal systems [47]. Moreover, Pb2+ exhibits greater removal across all three adsorbents compared to Cd2+, potentially due to several factors: (1) Pb2+ possesses a smaller hydrated radius (4.01 Å) than Cd2+ (4.26 Å); (2) Pb2+ exhibits higher electronegativity (2.33) compared to Cd2+ (1.69). The ratio of ametal (monometal adsorption) to a*metal (multimetal adsorption) elucidates the competitive adsorption effect on each metal's removal capacity. At solution concentration of 1 mmol/L, calculated ametal/a*metal ratios for different adsorbents consistently exceeded 1, indicating reduced removal capacity for each metal due to competitive adsorption sites in the presence of multiple metals. Remarkably, apb/a*pb > acd/a*cd suggests a more significant impact of competitive adsorption on Cd2+ than Pb2+ [37,48]. Additionally, Pb2+ demonstrated higher H0 compared to Cd2+ in single systems, enabling quicker attachment to active sites and consequently reducing available active sites for Cd2+ in the Supplementary Material (Table S6).

Multiply modified MgAl-LDH were prepared with CDs recombination, CA intercalation, and amino grafting by hydrothermal method. The characterization confirmed that the active sites for Pb2+ and Cd2+ were increased obviously. The removal capacity of Pb2+ and Cd2+ could reach 2.5 mmol/g and 1.5 mmol/g, respectively, under appropriate conditions. The adsorption fits best to the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and Langmuir isotherm model. After that, thermodynamic parameters confirmed the endothermic nature of the removal processes for adsorbents on Pb2+ and Cd2+. The Pb2+ and Cd2+ removal mechanisms by LCC-NH2 involved isomorphous substitution, chelation, and precipitation. Furthermore, the coexistence of multiple metal ions hindered the removal of Pb2+ and Cd2+ due to competitive adsorption, and the effect of competitive adsorption was more pronounced for Cd2+ than for Pb2+. In summary, LCC-NH2 emerges as a promising candidate for effective removal of heavy metal ions from aqueous solutions.

Deliang Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xiaorong Wei: Methodology, Formal analysis. Mengyu Lu: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Zhongying Shen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Quanwei Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Zhixian Chang: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Ruifeng Chong: Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support provided by the Key Research Project of Higher Education Institution of Henan Province (23A610014) and Open subject of Henan University (20232410240). Moreover, Special thanks go to Shufang Tian from Henan University for their significant help in this study.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidative ageing of biochar and hydrochar alleviating competitive sorption of Cd(II) and Cu(II)
Sci. Total Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, L. Wang, X. Wang, F. Jing, R. Chang, J. Chen, Oxidative ageing of biochar and hydrochar alleviating competitive sorption of Cd(II) and Cu(II), Sci. Total Environ. 725 (2020) 138419.
